summary: Uncle Bob responds to Erik Meijer's provocative conference talk 'One Hacker Way' with biting sarcasm, treating it as an elaborate hoax rather than serious advice. Meijer's talk advocated for skipping testing in favor of deploying to production and rolling back failures, quitting companies that practice TDD, organizing software teams like military hierarchies, and treating developers like professional athletes who should code intensely from ages 22-32 then retire. Uncle Bob systematically dismantles each argument by sarcastically agreeing with the absurd logical leaps—comparing the Catholic Church's longevity to the OSI stack, and equating programmers to world-cup soccer players. He concludes that the only explanation for such concatenated illogic is that Meijer brilliantly hoaxed the audience, who uncritically accepted the nonsense.
key_points: Meijer claimed TDD is waste and developers should deploy directly to production, rolling back when things fail
The talk advocated military-style hierarchical command structures for software teams, rejecting Agile
Meijer suggested programmers should work intensely only from ages 22-32, like professional athletes, then retire
Uncle Bob frames the entire talk as an elaborate hoax because he cannot accept anyone seriously believes these positions
The audience's uncritical acceptance of the arguments is presented as evidence of weak critical thinking
money_quotes: All this TDD crap? Forget about it. If your company does TDD what do you do? Leave! You quit! You hand in your resignation today!
Writing tests is waste. TDD is for pussies.
The church has been around for 2,000 years. No company has been around for 2,000 years. Why can the church be around so long? Because it's a layered architecture!
He's brilliant because he's pulled off the perfect hoax.
Listen as their weak minds uncritically seize upon his endless train of ridiculous confusion and nonsense.
Dr. Meijer filled forty-five minutes with absolute drivel, and made everybody love it. And that takes brilliance.
key_insight: When someone advocates abandoning testing, embracing rigid hierarchies, and treating programming as a young person's game, the ideas are so absurd they must be satire—and the real indictment is of audiences who accept them uncritically.
